Description: A machine learning model can detect anomalies in user login patterns that indicate compromised accounts. ML identifies unusual login patterns, such as unusual times, locations, or device types, which are strong indicators of account takeover attempts. By learning from recent login data, ML can adapt to detect sophisticated attacks, like credential stuffing or lateral movement, that might evade static detection rules. Compare current login behaviors against personalized user baselines and find potential compromise.
Data needed: AD logs, EDR logs, windows events

Use case: Beaconing behavior in DNS traffic (Detect C2 channels that evade signature-based detection)
Description: Detection of Cobalt Strike beaconing to a C2 server. Model flags hosts with near-constant intervals between outbound connections. Hunter investigates destination IP/domain reputation and process lineage.
Model used: time-series clustering or periodicity detection (e.g., Fourier Transform or ML-based sequence models) to network flows.

Use case: Data exfiltration detection with unusual outbound traffic volume
Description: Data exfiltration is a significant threat where sensitive information is transferred outside the organization. Analyzing byte sizes of outbound traffic can help detect unusual patterns that may indicate unauthorized data transfer. Correlate unusual byte sizes and spikes in outbound traffic volume during non-standard business hours focusing on file extensions known for containing sensitive information.

Machine learning models can identify database query anomalies indicating potential data manipulation or exfiltration activity. If a user or system executes an unusually high number of data modification queries (e.gl, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) within a short timeframe, particularly in sensitive databases, it may indicate potential data manipulation or exfiltration activities.

Use case: Identifying malicious base64 encoded payloads in scripts
Description: Encoded commands and scripts are more difficult to signature and analyze. Machine learning models can detect and decode Base64 commands, flag unusually long commands, and detect commands that match patterns of obfuscation. A number of adversaries have used Base64 to obfuscate commands and scripts, including APT19, Wizard Spider, and Fox Kitten. It is also a feature of remote access tools such as ComRAT and DarkWatchman.

Use case: Dictionary DGA detection via Supervised Classification
Description: Detect potential DNS tunneling and covert data exfiltration by identifying: Very long or high-entropy subdomains (data chunks), Excessive query volume to a single domain (QPS spikes), Periodic beaconing behavior, Abnormal query types often used for tunneling (TXT/NULL/HTTPS), High NXDOMAIN ratio (failed resolution as a potential covert channel)

Use case: Similarity Analysis via Clustering and Text-vectorization
Description: Data Collection and Preparation: Gather and encode data into numerical formats to support analysis (e.g., text vectorization or image hashing). Similarity Analysis: Use similarity metrics (e.g., Levenshtein, cosine, or hash-based) to find related patterns or anomalies. Clustering: Apply clustering (e.g., K-means) to group similar items, visualizing patterns and outliers. Prioritization and Investigation: Flag clusters or anomalies for deeper analysis, focusing on items of interest or risk.

Cluster process parent-child execution chains across the endpoint fleet to establish baseline lineage frequency, then identify rare or never-seen process trees that deviate from the norm to detect living-off-the-land abuse, novel malware execution, and compromised applications.

Train on host telemetry (processes, registry, network calls) to detect deviations in process or connection patterns that might indicate lateral pivoting or compromise. Note: make sure this is M-ATH and not Baseline hunt.
Use unsupervised models (e.g., clustering, isolation forests) to flag unusual flows, port usage, or spike patterns that may indicate stealthy communication.

An ML model learns the typical processes and command lines used on specific endpoints. Hunters investigate when: A normally quiet server suddenly launches scripting engines (PowerShell, Python), A process spawns an unusual child (e.g., winword.exe → powershell.exe), Exec frequency changes drastically within a short period.

Test a threat-informed hypothesis using a model to separate adversarial beaconing from legitimate periodic traffic. Periodic beaconing (jittered sleep), HTTP(S) with malleable profiles, Named pipe or SMB C2

Use case: Identify potential injections in User-agent string
Description: Create clusters of user-agent, URL length, number of variables passed to the URL, etc and isolate potential malicious/uncommon clusters

Hunt for processes using names of known legitimate binaries while exhibiting suspicious behaviors: executing from unusual or unexpected file paths, lacking valid digital signatures, spawning from atypical parent processes, or running with anomalous command-line arguments.

Use case: Threat Hunting Using Composite Scoring and VirusTotal Telemetry
Description: Apply a composite scoring model to VirusTotal command-line telemetry by combining multiple behavioral features into a single weighted score to help threat hunters prioritize novel or suspicious samples that might otherwise be lost in noisy data. This approach can surface complex, obfuscated commands and guide analysis better than simple detection counts, and explains how the methodology can be extended to internal telemetry for proactive hunting workflows.

To aid analysts in identifying techniques which may be co-occurring as part of a campaign, we present the Technique Inference Engine, a tool to infer tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) which may be related to existing observations of adversarial behavior. We compile the largest (to our knowledge) available dataset of cyber threat intelligence (CTI) reports labeled with relevant TTPs. With the knowledge that techniques are chronically under-reported in CTI, we apply several implicit feedback recommender models to the data in order to predict additional techniques which may be part of a given campaign. We evaluate the results in the context of the cyber analyst's use case and apply t-SNE to visualize the model embeddings.
Model used: Supercharging your threat hunting with LLMs: How to do it efficiently and on a budget

Description: The technique aids in identifying the first time a specific file (SHA-256 hash or file name) is executed anywhere in the organization. By baselining unique hashes seen across the entire assets fleet over 30-45 days, low prevelance binaries that appear on 1-2 machines can be isolated. These low-prevelance binaries could mean custom compiled malware, unauthorized tools (remote connection) or unidentified binary (antivirus signature).
As most malware want to achieve persistence on the victim systems, the focus is here on the Registry and File System locations that Windows uses to automatically start programs.

Use case: Suspicious browser extensions to collect and exfiltrate sensitive data
Description: Browser extension or VS Code extensions

Punycode-encoded International Domain Names (IDNs) with the "xn--" prefix in DNS queries to masquerade visually similar domain names that impersonate legitimate services for credential harvesting and malware delivery

This use case focuses on detecting abuse of VPN/remote access after credential theft or by insiders, using user behavior analytics on enriched VPN and auth logs. The model learns each user's "normal" geo, time, and data‑volume patterns, then flags anomalies such as unusual or first‑time countries/ASNs, impossible‑travel logins, atypical hours (e.g. 3AM for a 9–5 user), and abnormal post‑VPN data transfer, often combining time‑series and geo‑anomaly analysis on VPN, authentication, and NetFlow logs with Geo‑IP enrichment.

Reads CSV files from input/ (recursively) and flags potentially suspicious DNS questions and abnormal URL access. Uses rule-based scoring (Shannon entropy, suspicious keywords, risky TLDs, punycode/base64 decoding) and detection logics to surface anomalies.

Add details on network beaconing detection with randomized (jittered) timing patterns. Discuss the challenges of identifying periodic C2 beacons when attackers use jitter to evade fixed-interval detection, and suggest ML or rule-based approaches to address this evasion technique.

Use case: Abnormal DLL loading in common applications (e.g. Teams, Edge)
Description: Hunt for DLL or module loads that are unusual for high-prevalence user applications such as Microsoft Teams, Microsoft Edge, and other common IM or browser processes. Baseline typical image paths, publishers, and load frequency for each process across the fleet, then prioritize rare paths (e.g. user profile, temp, non-standard app directories), unsigned or low-prevalence binaries, side-loading adjacent to legitimate binaries, and loads that diverge from peer hosts. Useful for DLL hijacking, search-order hijacking, and blending malicious code with trusted application activity.
Model used: frequency or prevalence baselining; rare-path or rare-module scoring; optional peer-based anomaly comparison
Data needed: EDR module/image load telemetry (e.g. Sysmon Event ID 7); Windows process loading events

Description: Detect process memory injection techniques such as Process Hollowing, DLL Injection, and reflective DLL loading by isolating processes that exhibit anomalous memory allocation profiles. By monitoring dynamic memory adjustments on endpoints, threat hunters can uncover stealthy implants, packing, and beaconing code that bypass traditional on-disk antivirus checks.
Model used: Unsupervised Clustering (e.g. K-Means, DBSCAN), Anomaly Detection (e.g. Isolation Forest, Autoencoders), Feature extraction from endpoint memory telemetry includes page protection transitions (e.g. RWX/RX allocation status), total allocation sizes, memory page state, and thread start addresses referencing unmapped memory. These features are scored using an Isolation Forest to rank anomalous process allocations, or clustered using DBSCAN to isolate rare execution profiles across host clusters.
Data needed: Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) logs, Windows Event logs, Windows Sysmon Event ID 8 (CreateRemoteThread) and Event ID 10 (ProcessAccess); Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (MDE) DeviceEvents (ActionTypes: CreateRemoteThreadApiCall, NtMapViewOfSectionRemoteApiCall, QueueUserApcApiCall, OpenProcessApiCall, VirtualAllocApiCall, VirtualProtectApiCall); CrowdStrike Falcon Event Streams (event_simpleName: InjectedThread, ProcessRollup2); SentinelOne Deep Visibility (event.type: Remote Thread Creation, Open Remote Process Handle, Process Modification); or OS-native memory allocation events (e.g., Event Tracing for Windows - ETW).
Source: MITRE ATT&CK T1055 (Process Injection), elastic guide to threat hunting in-memory detection.

Use case: GitHub PAT reuse in anomalous network context
Description: Detect potential theft and reuse of GitHub Personal Access Tokens by baselining each PAT's normal countries, network carriers/ASNs, and user-agent families, then flagging suspicious reuse from unusual geolocation and client context. Includes travel-velocity checks between consecutive token uses and score-based prioritization for takeover-like activity.
Data needed: GitHub audit logs / PAT usage telemetry
